message:
test(runtime): treat raw output/log drift as non-blocking for non-strict assertions

Extends the `shadow_run_comparator` harness so that raw
`logs` / `terminal_output` field drift is only blocking for the
strictest assertion category.

## Behavior

A new `mismatch_is_blocking(assertion, mismatch)` helper returns
`true` for every mismatch under `AssertionCategory::NoChange` — the
strict parity category — and returns `true` for every mismatch
under any of `NoRegression`, `BoundedDegradation`, `GracefulFallback`,
`FailureForensics`, or `Improvement` *except* when the mismatch
field is `"logs"` or `"terminal_output"`, which are now treated as
acceptable drift under those categories. The companion
`blocking_mismatch_count(assertion, mismatches)` returns the number
of mismatches that remain after that filter.

`build_scenario_report` now computes `blocking_mismatches` via that
helper and uses it (instead of `mismatches.is_empty()`) to decide
`verdict` (`match` vs `diverged`) and `contract_status`
(`within-contract` vs `out-of-contract`).

The `acceptable_difference_policy` string is no longer a single
catch-all — it now switches on `spec.assertion` and emits
category-specific wording so a reader of the report understands
exactly which fields are allowed to drift for the current scenario:

- NoChange: exact lane parity required across trace, output, logs,
  state, policy, and frame captures.
- GracefulFallback: raw terminal/log-output drift is tolerated under
  bursty saturation, but trace / frame-hash / running-state /
  tick-rate / command-count / replay-context mismatches still block.
- NoRegression / BoundedDegradation / FailureForensics / Improvement:
  same as GracefulFallback but framed as "operator workloads may
  tolerate ... scheduling differences".

## Why

Scheduling differences between the legacy and structured runtime
lanes produce tiny, non-semantic terminal-output / log-line
differences in practice — e.g. timing-driven interleavings in log
output that are not contract violations. Counting those as blocking
mismatches hid *real* regressions behind a wall of noise and made
the `diverged` verdict meaningless for non-strict scenarios.

A new positive-case test `graceful_fallback_terminal_output_only_is_non_blocking`
verifies the new classification so a future refactor cannot silently
re-enable the old, noisier behavior.
